title: Emergent flocking dynamics in chemorepulsive active colloids: interplay of disorder and noise
abstract: Recent studies of active colloidal matter have revealed that a global polar order can arise from chemorepulsive interactions among particles without any explicit alignment interaction between them. In this work, we investigate such chemically interacting active colloids in the presence of quenched disorder, where a fraction of particles are randomly pinned in space. These pinned particles are restricted to rotational motion while remaining chemically coupled to the mobile population. In addition, angular noise is incorporated into the rotational dynamics to capture stochastic effects. To elucidate the interplay of quenched disorder and noise, we construct phase diagrams based on polar order and its fluctuations, and systematically analyze the associated disorder- and noise-driven phase transitions. Surprisingly, we find that the phase transition driven by the noise is significantly dependent on the density of the particles, whereas such a density-dependence is not present when the control parameter is the pinning fraction. The finite-size effects on these transitions are also examined. An effective interaction range, governed by the coefficient related to screening of the chemorepulsive interaction, plays a crucial role in collective behavior. When the effective interaction range is much smaller than the system size, the system exhibits density band formation, a feature absent in the long-range interaction regime. Moreover, near the transition point, the order parameter distribution becomes bimodal for the case of short-range interaction.
summary: This paper studies how noise and quenched disorder compete to influence flocking in active matter with chemical interactions. It reveals that the nature of the phase transition and the formation of spatial patterns like density bands are highly sensitive to the range of the chemical interaction.
main_problem: Investigating how collective flocking dynamics and spatial order emerge and are modified by the interplay of quenched disorder (pinned particles) and angular noise in chemorepulsive active colloids.
main_result: The noise-driven phase transition depends significantly on particle density, whereas the pinning-driven transition does not; additionally, short-range interactions lead to discontinuous transitions and density band formation.
method: Particle-based numerical simulations in a 2D continuous space using an Euler-Maruyama integrator and finite-size scaling analysis.
model_or_system: A 2D collection of active colloids consisting of mobile and pinned particles that interact via a self-generated, chemorepulsive chemical field with both translational and rotational components.
key_observables: Polar order parameter (m), hexatic order parameter (psi_6), susceptibility (chi), Binder cumulant (U), and order parameter distribution P(m).
important_parameters: Pinning fraction (np), angular noise strength (Lambda_n), interaction screening parameter (lambda), and particle area fraction (phi).
main_assumptions: The chemical field is modeled in the steady-state limit (instantaneous interaction), and particles have a fixed radius and constant self-propulsion speed.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows model schematics; Figure 2 presents phase diagrams for order parameters; Figure 4 illustrates phase transitions and density bands; Figure 5 shows order parameter probability distributions; Figure 6 shows radial pair correlation functions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the chemorepulsive model, presents phase diagrams for noise-free/disorder-free systems, analyzes the impact of noise and pinning disorder on phase transitions, explores the effect of interaction range (long vs. short), and concludes with scaling and structural analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic description of the model is shown in panel (a). Free and pinned active particles are randomly distributed initially in a two-dimensional space. Free and pinned particles are represented, respectively, by yellow and gray colour, red arrows indicates their orientation. (b) Noisy chemorepulsive interaction between two free active particles. (c) Noisy chemorepulsive interaction between a free active particle and a pinned active particle.

FIG. 2. Without quenched disorder and noise: (a) Phase diagram of polar order; crystallite flock: m > 0.6, Random: m < 0.1 in the (Λr, Λt) plane; (b) phase diagram of Hexatic order parameter ψ6 in the same plane. Fixed parameters: L = 200, ϕ = 0.1, np = 0, Λn = 0, and λ = 0.01.

FIG. 3. Here, λ = 0.01. (a) Phase diagram of m in the (np, Λn) plane, corresponding (b) fluctuations of order parameter (susceptibility χ). Fixed parameters: L = 200, Λr = 0.1, Λt = 0.1, ϕ = 0.1. The continuous orange colored line in (b) shows the transition line approximately. The blue dotted line represents transition line for higher density with ϕ = 0.36. Steady-state configuration with (c) Λn = 0, np = 0, (d) Λn = 0.0003, np = 0.1, for total number of particles N = 4000. Pinned particles are marked in gray color and direction of motion of the free particles follows the color scheme as shown in the middle of (c) and (d). Finite size effects on phase transition (with Λn = 0): (e) order...

FIG. 4. Here λ = 0.5. (a) Phase diagram of m in the (np, Λn) plane, corresponding (b) fluctuations of order parameter (susceptibility χ). Fixed parameters: L = 200, Λr = 0.1, Λt = 0.1, ϕ = 0.1. The continuous orange curve is fitted approximately to show the transition line. The blue dotted line in (b) represents transition line for higher density with ϕ = 0.36. Steady-state configuration with (c) Λn = 0.0008, np = 0, (d) Λn = 0, np = 0.16, for total number of particles N = 4000. Pinned particles are marked in gray color and direction of motion of the free particles follows the color scheme as shown by the color bar. Finite size effects on phase transition (with Λn = 0): (e) order parameter...

FIG. 5. Order parameter distribution at transition point. Left panel: effective long-ranged interaction with co-efficient λ = 0.01 (a) for control parameter np(= 0.22), (c) for control parameter Λn(= 0.0016). Right panel: effective short-ranged interaction with co-efficient λ = 0.5 (b) for control parameter np(= 0.23), (d) for control parameter Λn(= 0.00144). System size L = 400; area fraction ϕ = 0.1.
